content.body: Electrolysis is the process of using electricity to break down an ionic compound (electrolyte) into its elements. It requires a direct current (DC) passed through a molten ionic compound or ionic solution. Electrolysis is used in electroplating, purifying metals, and industrial production of chemicals.

Key components of electrolysis:
Electrolyte: the ionic compound being decomposed (molten or in solution)
Anode (+): positive electrode — OXIDATION occurs (lose electrons)
Cathode (−): negative electrode — REDUCTION occurs (gain electrons)
Electrodes: conductors (usually graphite or platinum)

Mnemonic: OIL RIG
Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons)
Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)

content.title: Electrolysis of Molten Lead Bromide
content.body:
Lead bromide (PbBr₂) — molten:
Ions present: Pb²⁺ and Br⁻

At cathode (−): Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb (lead deposited)
At anode (+): 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻ (bromine gas produced)

Products: Lead metal at cathode, bromine gas at anode
This shows electrolysis can extract metals from compounds.

content.title: Electrolysis of Copper Sulphate Solution
content.body:
CuSO₄ solution with copper electrodes (used for electroplating):

At cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper deposited on cathode)
At anode: Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (copper anode dissolves)

Result: Copper transfers from anode to cathode!
Used to purify copper and for electroplating.

With inert (graphite) electrodes in CuSO₄:
Cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (deposited)
Anode: 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ (oxygen released)

Industrial applications of electrolysis:
1. Aluminium extraction (Hall-Héroult process): Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite → electrolysis → pure Al
2. Chlor-alkali industry: electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution) → Cl₂ gas (anode), H₂ gas (cathode), NaOH solution
3. Electroplating: depositing thin layer of metal on another → decorative, protective (chrome bumpers, gold jewellery)
4. Copper purification: impure copper anode, pure copper cathode, CuSO₄ electrolyte

questions.question: At which electrode does reduction occur?
questions.explanation: Reduction (gain of electrons) occurs at the cathode (negative electrode). OIL RIG — Reduction Is Gain.

questions.question: OIL RIG stands for:
options: Oxygen In Liquids, Reduction In Gases
Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain
Only Ions Leave, Reduce If Gained
Oxygen Is Liquid, Reduced Into Gas
questions.answer: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain
questions.explanation: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).

questions.question: In electrolysis of molten NaCl, what forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: Na⁺ ions migrate to cathode (−): Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na. Sodium metal is deposited.

questions.question: Electrolysis requires the electrolyte to be:
options: Molten or dissolved (ions must be free to move)
questions.answer: Molten or dissolved (ions must be free to move)
questions.explanation: Electrolysis requires free-moving ions to carry current. Ions are only free in molten state or aqueous solution.

questions.question: Electroplating uses electrolysis to:
options: Deposit a thin metal layer on an object
questions.answer: Deposit a thin metal layer on an object
questions.explanation: Electroplating deposits a thin layer of one metal onto another — for decoration, protection, or reduced cost.

questions.question: In electrolysis of CuSO₄ with copper electrodes:
options: Copper transfers from anode to cathode
questions.answer: Copper transfers from anode to cathode
questions.explanation: Copper anode dissolves (Cu→Cu²⁺+2e⁻) and Cu²⁺ deposits at cathode. Net effect: copper transfers from anode to cathode.

questions.question: Industrial aluminium is extracted by:
options: Electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ (dissolved in cryolite)
questions.answer: Electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ (dissolved in cryolite)
questions.explanation: Al is too reactive to extract by carbon reduction. Electrolysis of molten aluminium oxide (Hall-Héroult process) is used.

questions.question: The three products of brine electrolysis are:
questions.explanation: Electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution): Cl₂ gas at anode, H₂ gas at cathode, NaOH solution remains.

questions.question: Why can't solid NaCl be electrolysed?
options: Ions are fixed in the lattice and cannot move
questions.answer: Ions are fixed in the lattice and cannot move
questions.explanation: In solid NaCl, Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions are fixed. Current cannot flow. The solid must be melted or dissolved to free the ions.

questions.question: At the anode during electrolysis of molten PbBr₂:
questions.explanation: Anode is positive. Br⁻ ions are attracted to it: 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻. Oxidation (loss of electrons) at anode.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of chemical reactions — calculating how much of each reactant is needed and how much product is formed. The mole is the central unit for chemical calculations.

The Mole (mol):
1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number)

Molar mass (M): mass of 1 mole in grams = same number as relative atomic/formula mass
C = 12 g/mol, O = 16 g/mol, H = 1 g/mol, Na = 23 g/mol, Cl = 35.5 g/mol

Key formula:
Moles = Mass / Molar mass
n = m / M

Moles = Volume (L) × Concentration (mol/L)
n = V × c

content.title: Mole Calculations
content.body:
How many moles in 44g of CO₂?
Molar mass of CO₂ = 12 + (2×16) = 44 g/mol
n = 44/44 = 1 mole

Mass of 2 moles of NaCl?
Molar mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 g/mol
Mass = 2 × 58.5 = 117 g

How many molecules in 0.5 mol of H₂O?
Number = 0.5 × 6.02×10²³ = 3.01×10²³ molecules

Using Chemical Equations for Calculations:
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O

This means: 2 mol H₂ reacts with 1 mol O₂ to form 2 mol H₂O
Mole ratios: H₂:O₂:H₂O = 2:1:2

Steps for stoichiometry problems:
1. Write balanced equation
2. Write moles of known substance (n = m/M)
3. Use mole ratio to find moles of unknown
4. Convert moles to mass, volume, or particles

content.title: Stoichiometry Problem
content.body:
How much CaO forms when 50g of CaCO₃ is heated?
CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂

Step 1: Balanced (already 1:1:1)
Step 2: n(CaCO₃) = 50/100 = 0.5 mol
         (M of CaCO₃ = 40+12+48 = 100 g/mol)
Step 3: n(CaO) = 0.5 mol (1:1 ratio)
Step 4: mass(CaO) = 0.5 × 56 = 28 g
         (M of CaO = 40+16 = 56 g/mol)

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: 1 mole contains 6.02 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's constant).

questions.question: Molar mass of H₂O (H=1, O=16):
n = m/M. Moles of 24g Mg (M=24):
Moles of 36g H₂O:
Mass of 3 mol of NaOH (M=40):
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Moles of H₂O from 4 mol H₂:
questions.explanation: Ratio H₂:H₂O = 2:2 = 1:1. 4 mol H₂ → 4 mol H₂O.

questions.question: CaCO₃→CaO+CO₂. Mass of CO₂ from 100g CaCO₃ (M=100,44):
questions.explanation: n(CaCO₃)=1mol. 1:1 ratio → n(CO₂)=1mol. m=1×44=44g.

questions.question: Concentration 2 mol/L, volume 0.5L. Moles = ?
M of H₂SO₄ (H=1, S=32, O=16):
Number of molecules in 2 mol of CO₂:
questions.explanation: 2 × 6.02×10²³ = 1.204×10²⁴ molecules.

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Electrolysis is used in industry for: electroplating (coating objects with metal), refining metals (purifying copper), extracting reactive metals (aluminium from aluminium oxide), and manufacturing chemicals (chlorine from brine).

content.title: Extraction of Aluminium (Hall-Héroult Process)
content.body:
Electrolyte: molten aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) in cryolite
Cathode (−): Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al (liquid aluminium settles at bottom)
Anode (+): 2O²⁻ → O₂ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas released, reacts with carbon anode)
Anodes must be replaced regularly as they burn away
Highly energy intensive — aluminium is expensive to produce

content.title: Electrolysis of Brine (NaCl solution)
content.body:
Products: Cl₂ gas (anode), H₂ gas (cathode), NaOH solution
Cathode: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂
Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻
Chlorine → PVC, bleach, disinfectant
Hydrogen → fuel, margarine production
NaOH → soap, paper making

Electroplating:
Object to be plated = cathode
Plating metal = anode
Electrolyte = solution of plating metal salt

Example: Copper plating
Anode: Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (dissolves)
Cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (deposits on object)

questions.question: In electrolysis, which electrode is the positive one?
questions.explanation: The anode is the positive electrode. Anions (negative ions) are attracted to it and are oxidised there.

questions.question: Aluminium is extracted from molten aluminium oxide (not aqueous solution) because...
options: Al³⁺ ions need to be mobile; in solution water would be reduced instead
questions.answer: Al³⁺ ions need to be mobile; in solution water would be reduced instead
questions.explanation: If aqueous: H₂O is more easily reduced than Al³⁺. Molten Al₂O₃ provides mobile Al³⁺ ions without water competing.

questions.question: In Hall-Héroult process, carbon anodes must be regularly replaced because...
options: They react with oxygen produced and burn away
questions.answer: They react with oxygen produced and burn away
questions.explanation: O²⁻ is oxidised to O₂ at anode. This hot O₂ reacts with the carbon electrode: C + O₂ → CO₂, burning it away.

questions.question: Electrolysis of brine produces which three products?
questions.explanation: Brine electrolysis: Cl₂ gas at anode, H₂ gas at cathode, NaOH solution remains.

questions.question: In electroplating, the object to be plated is connected as...
questions.explanation: Metal ions from solution are reduced and deposited at cathode. Object = cathode → coated in metal.

questions.question: Chlorine produced from brine is used to make...
questions.explanation: Chlorine is a highly useful industrial chemical: makes PVC plastic, bleach (NaOCl), disinfectants, HCl.

questions.question: In copper purification by electrolysis...
questions.answer: Impure Cu anode dissolves, pure Cu deposits on cathode
questions.explanation: Impure copper anode dissolves → Cu²⁺ ions in solution → pure copper deposits at cathode. Impurities fall as anode sludge.

questions.question: Faraday's laws state that the mass of substance deposited is proportional to...
questions.explanation: Faraday's first law: m ∝ Q = It. More charge → more ions discharged → more mass deposited.

questions.question: Why is aluminium production very expensive?
options: It requires enormous amounts of electrical energy
questions.answer: It requires enormous amounts of electrical energy
questions.explanation: Hall-Héroult electrolysis runs at very high temperatures and currents. Electrical energy cost is the main expense in Al production.

questions.question: The role of cryolite in aluminium production is to...
options: Lower the melting point of Al₂O₃ from 2072°C to ~950°C
questions.answer: Lower the melting point of Al₂O₃ from 2072°C to ~950°C
questions.explanation: Pure Al₂O₃ melts at 2072°C — impractical and very expensive. Dissolved in cryolite, melting point drops to ~950°C.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electrochemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electrochemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Electrochemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electrochemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electrochemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Electrochemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electrochemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electrochemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electrochemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Electrochemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electrochemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electrochemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Electrochemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electrochemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
